topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the cold finality of a closing door. Professor Albright was departing. My stomach immediately knotted, a tight, unfamiliar band around my middle. I reread the message three times, the words blurring slightly as I focused on the stark reality: my thesis supervisor was leaving. Panic began to bubble up, hot and frantic, making my hands tremble as I gripped the mouse. I needed to call him, to ask if this was temporary, if he would still be around for my data analysis. But the silence of my room was deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. Every shadow in the corner of my desk seemed to shift, and I couldn't seem to settle my breathing.

[story 2]

I watched the clock tick toward midnight, the fluorescent lights of the library casting a sickly yellow glow on my laptop screen. The news—that Dr. Vance was stepping down—had hit me like a physical blow. My palms grew slick against the keyboard; I had to stop typing, my fingers hovering uselessly above the keys. A wave of intense unease washed over me, making the air feel thick and hard to draw in. I stood up abruptly, pacing the narrow aisle between the study carrels. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic rhythm I couldn't control. What would happen to my progress? The entire structure of my research felt suddenly precarious, balanced on a thread about to snap.

[story 3]

When I finally saw the announcement posted on the departmental bulletin board, the sound seemed to drain out of the room. It was Albright. Gone. A profound stillness settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I felt a sudden, sharp constriction in my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. My gaze darted around the empty office, cataloging the familiar objects—the stack of annotated papers, the worn coffee mug—and suddenly they looked alien, charged with a sense of abandonment. I pressed my thighs together, a futile attempt to anchor myself to the floor. My throat tightened, and I could only manage a shallow, ragged inhale.
